classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Ye men of Israel, hear these my words.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: : it is not for flattery that he uses this term; but, as he has borne hard upon them, he relaxes a little, and puts them in mind of their great ancestor [Israel].

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here again he begins with an introduction, that they may not become excited, now that he is going to make express mention to them of Jesus: for in what preceded, there was no reason why they should be excited, while the Prophet was the subject of discourse: but the name of Jesus would have given offence at the very outset.—And he does not say, “Do as I bid you,” but, Hear; as being not at all exacting.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe how he forbears to speak of the high matters, and begins with the very low: “Jesus,” he says: and then straightway mentions the place He belonged to, being one which was held in mean estimation: “Jesus of Nazareth”: and does not say anything great about Him, nor even such as one would say about a Prophet, so far: “Jesus,” he says, “of Nazareth, a man proved (to be) from God among you.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe; what great matter was this, to say that He was sent from God?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this was the point which on all occasions both He and John and the Apostles were studious to show.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus hear John saying: “The same said unto me On whom thou shalt see the Spirit descending, and abiding on him, this is He.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But Christ Himself does this to an extreme; Of Myself I am not come, He sent Me. (ib. vii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And everywhere in the Scriptures this seems the point most studiously insisted upon.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore also this holy leader of the blessed company, the lover of Christ, the good shepherd, the man put in trust with the keys of heaven, the man who received the Spiritual Wisdom, when he has first subdued the Jews by fear; and has shown what great things have been vouchsafed to the disciples, and what a right they have to be believed, then first proceeds to speak concerning Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Only think what boldness it was to say it, in the midst of the murderers—that He is risen!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet he does not all at once say, He is risen; but what?—“He came,” says he, “from God: this is manifest by the signs which”—he does not yet say, Jesus Himself wrought: but what?—“which God wrought by Him in the midst of you.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He calls themselves as witnesses.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “A man proved (to be sent) from God among you, by miracles and wonders and signs, which God wrought by him in the midst of you, as also ye yourselves know.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then, having fallen upon the mention of that their sacrilegious outrage, observe how he endeavors to quit them of the crime: “Him,” he says, “being by the determinate counsel and foreknowledge of God delivered up”: (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 23) [adding however,] “ye have taken, and by wicked hands have crucified and slain:” for though it was predetermined, still they were murderers. [“By the determinate counsel and foreknowledge of God:”] all but using the same words as Joseph did; just as he said to his brethren; “Be not angry one with another by the way: God sent me hither.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is God’s doing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “What of us, then?” (it might be said,) “it was even well done on our part.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That they may not say this, therefore it is that he adds, “By wicked hands ye have crucified and slain.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here then he hints at Judas; while at the same time he shows them that it was not from any strength of theirs, and would not have been, if He had not Himself permitted it: it was God that delivered Him up.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He has transferred the evil entire upon the head of Judas, now already parted from them; for he it was that delivered Him over to them by the kiss.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Or, “By wicked hands,” refers to the soldiers: for neither is it simply, “Ye have slain,” but, By wicked men ye have done this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe how everywhere they make it of great importance that the Passion should first be confessed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whom God Raised Up (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 24), says he.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This was the great thing; and observe how he sets it in the middle of his discourse: for the former matters had been confessed; both the miracles and the signs and the slaying—“Whom God,” says he, “raised up, having loosed the pains of death, because it was not possible that He should be kept in its power.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is something great and sublime that he has hinted at here.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the expression, “It was not possible,” even itself is that of one assigning something.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It shows that death itself in holding Him had pangs as in travail, and was sore bestead: whereas, by pains, or, travail-pangs, of death, the Old Testament means danger and disaster: and that He so rose as never more to die.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the assertion, “Seeing that it was not possible that He should be holden of it,” means this, that His rising was not common to the rest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then, however, before their thoughts can enter at all into his meaning, he brings David upon them, an authority which sets aside all human reasoning.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For David saith (with reference) to Him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe how, once more, the testimony is lowly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For therefore he begins the citation further up, with the matters of lowlier import: therefore was death not in the number of grievous things [because], says he, “I foresaw the Lord always before my face, that He is on my right hand that I should not be moved:” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 25–27) and, “that Thou wilt not leave my soul in hell.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then, having finished the citation from the Prophet, he adds; “Men and brethren.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When he is about to say anything great, he uses this opening address, to rouse and to conciliate them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Let me be allowed,” he says, “to speak freely to you of the patriarch David.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Remarkable lowliness, in a case where he was giving no hurt, nor was there any reason why the hearers should be angry.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he did not say, This is not said concerning David, but concerning the Christ.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But in another point of view: by his reverential expression towards the blessed David, he awed them; speaking of an acknowledged fact as if it were a bold thing to say, and therefore begging them to pardon him for saying it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And thereupon his expression is not simply “concerning David,” but “concerning the patriarch David, that he is both dead and buried:” he does not also say, “and is not risen again,” but in another way (though this too would have been no great thing to say), “And his sepulchre is with us unto this day,” he has said what comes to the same thing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then—and even so he does not come to the mention of Christ, but what next?—he goes on with his encomium upon David, “Being therefore a prophet, and knowing that with an oath God had sworn unto him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But this he says, that were it but on account of the honor shown to David, and the descent from him, they may accept what is said concerning Christ’s resurrection, as seeing that it would be an injury to the prophecy, and a derogating from (τἥς εἰς αὐτοὺς τιμἥς) their honor, if this were not the fact.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And knowing,” he says, “that with an oath God had sworn unto him”—he does not say simply “promised”—“of the fruit of his loins after the flesh to raise up Christ, to seat Him upon his throne.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how he has again only hinted at what is sublime.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For now that he has soothed them with his expression, he confidently adds this: The prophet [saith it] “of His resurrection, that neither was His soul left in hell, nor did His flesh see corruption.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This again is wonderful: it shows that His resurrection was not like that of other men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For though death laid hold on Him, yet it did not its own work then.—And, as regards the sin, he has spoken of that, covertly and darkly; of the punishment, he forbore to add anything; but that they had slain Him, this he has spoken out; for the rest he now comes to the sign given by God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when it is once proved, that He, the slain, was just, was dear to God, then, though thou be silent of the punishment, be sure that he which did the sin will condemn himself more than ever thou canst condemn him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So then, that he refers all to the Father, is in order that they may receive what is said: and that assertion, “Not possible,” he fetches in from the prophecy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well then, let us again look over what has been said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Jesus of Nazareth, a man proved (to be sent) from God unto you.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation of v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 22–31): one, of whom, by reason of His works, there can be no doubt; but who, on the contrary, is demonstrated.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus also Nicodemus said, “No man can do these miracles which Thou doest—By miracles, and wonders, and signs which God wrought by Him in the midst of you” (John iii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 2): not secretly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Setting out from facts notorious to those whom he was addressing, he then comes to things hidden.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thereupon [in saying, “By the determinate counsel and foreknowledge of God,”] (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 23) he shows that it was not because they had the power to do it, and that there was a wisdom and a Divine arrangement in the event, seeing it was from God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He rapidly passes over the unpleasant part, [adding, “Whom God raised up,” etc.] (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is always a point of great importance with them to show that He was once dead.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Though ye should deny it, says he, (ἐκεῖνοι) those (present) will bear witness to the fact. [“Having loosed the pangs of death.”]

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He that gives Death trouble, may much more give trouble to them that crucified Him: however, nothing of the kind is here said, as that He had power to slay you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Meanwhile, let us also learn thus to hold.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For one that is in pain like a woman in travail, does not hold the thing held, and is not active but passive; and makes haste to cast it off.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And it is well said: “For David saith in reference to him” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 25); that you may not refer that saying to the Prophet.—[“Therefore being a Prophet, and knowing,” etc.] (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you observe how he now interprets the prophecy, and does not give it bare of comment?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How did He “seat Him upon” David’s “throne?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the kingdom after the Spirit is in heaven.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how, along with the resurrection, he has also declared the kingdom in the fact of His rising again.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He shows that the Prophet was under constraint: for the prophecy was concerning Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why does he say, not, Concerning His kingdom (it was a great matter), but “Concerning His resurrection?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And how did He seat Him upon his (David’s) throne?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, He reigns as King over Jews also, yea, what is much more, over them that crucified Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For His flesh saw no corruption.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This seems to be less than resurrection, but it is the same thing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “This Jesus”—observe how he does not call Him otherwise—“hath God raised up; whereof all we are witnesses.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Being therefore by the right hand of God exalted” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 33, 34): again he takes refuge with the Father, and yet it had been enough to say what precedes: but he knows what a great point this is.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here he has hinted at the Ascension also, and that Christ is in heaven: but neither does he say this openly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And having received,” says he, “the promise of the Holy Ghost.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how, in the beginning of his discourse, he does not say that Jesus Himself had sent It, but the Father: now, however, that he has mentioned His signs and the things done to Him by the Jews, and has spoken of His resurrection, he boldly introduces what he has to say about these matters, again adducing themselves as witnesses by both senses: [“He hath shed forth this, which ye do see and hear.”]

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And of the resurrection he has made continual mention, but of their outrageous deed he has spoken once for all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And having received the promise of the Holy Ghost.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This again is great.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The promise,” he says; because [promised] before His Passion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how he now makes it all His [“He hath poured forth this”], covertly making a great point.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if it was He that poured it forth, it is of Him that the Prophet has spoken above, “In the last days I will pour forth of My Spirit on My Servants, and on Mine handmaids, and I will do wonders in the heaven above. (supra, v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe what he secretly puts into it!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But then, because it was a great thing, he again veils it with the expression of “His having received of the Father.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He has spoken of the good things fulfilled, of the signs; has said, that He is king, the point that touched them; has said, that it is He that gives the Spirit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (For, however much a person may say, if it does not issue in something advantageous, he speaks to no purpose.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Just as John: “The same,” says he, “shall baptize you with the Holy Ghost.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And it shows that the Cross not only did not make Him less, but rendered Him even more illustrious, seeing that of old God promised it to Him, but now has given it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Or [it may be], “the promise” which He promised to us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He so foreknew it about to be, and has given it to us greater after the resurrection.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And, “hath poured it out,” he says; not requiring worthiness: and not simply gave, but with abundance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whence does this appear?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Henceforth after the mention of His giving the Spirit, he confidently speaks also of His ascension into heaven; and not only so, but again adducing the witness, and reminding them of that Person concerning Whom Christ once spake.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Matt. xxii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 43) “For not David,” says he “ascended into the heavens. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here he no longer speaks in lowly phrase, having the confidence which results from the things said; nor does he say, “Be it permitted me to speak,” or the like: “But he saith himself; The Lord said unto my Lord, Sit Thou on My right hand, until I make Thine enemies Thy footstool.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now if He be David’s Lord, much more shall they not disdain Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Sit thou on My right hand;” he has set the whole matter here; “until I make Thine enemies Thy footstool:” here also he has brought upon them a great terror, just as in the beginning he showed what He does to His friends, what to his enemies.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And again, as to the act of subjugation, not to provoke unbelief, he ascribes it to the Father.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since then these are great things that he has uttered, he again brings his discourse down to lowly matters.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Let therefore,” he says, “the whole house of Israel know assuredly: i.e. question ye not, nor doubt ye: then also in the tone of command it follows; “that God hath made Him both Lord”—this he says from David—“and Christ,” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 36), this from the Psalm: For when it would have been rightly concluded, “Let therefore the whole house of Israel know assuredly that” He sitteth on the right hand of God, this, which would have been great, he forbears, and brings in a different matter which is much more humble, and the expression “Hath made;” i.e. hath ordained: so that there is nothing about (οὐσίωσις) communication of substance here, but the expression relates to this which has been mentioned.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Even this Jesus, Whom ye crucified.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He does well to end with this, thereby agitating their minds.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For when he has shown how great it is, he has then exposed their daring deed, so as to show it to be greater, and to possess them with terror.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For men are not so much attracted by benefits as they are chastened by fear.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But the admirable and great ones, and beloved of God, need none of these motives: men, such as was Paul: not of the kingdom, not of hell, made he account.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this is indeed to love Christ, this to be no hireling, nor to reckon it a matter of trafficking and trading, but to be indeed virtuous, and to do all for the love of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then what tears does it not deserve, when, owing so large a measure, we do not even like traders seek the kingdom of heaven!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He promises us so great things, and not even so is He worthy to be heard?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What can come up to this enmity!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet, they are mad after money-making, though it be with enemies, though it be with slaves, though it be with persons most hostile to them, that they come in contact, though it be with persons utterly evil, if only they expect that they shall be enabled by their means to make money, they will do everything, will flatter, and be obsequious, and make themselves slaves, and will esteem them more to be revered than all men, to get some advantage out of them: for the hope of money does not allow them to give a thought to any such considerations as these.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But the Kingdom is not so powerful as money is; nay, rather, not in the smallest proportion as powerful.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is no ordinary Being that promises: but this is greater than even the Kingdom itself that we receive it from such a Giver!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But now the case is the same as if a king, wishing, after ten thousand other benefits, to make us his heirs and coheirs with his son [should be despised]: while some captain of a band of robbers, who has done ten thousand wrongs to us and to our parents, and is himself fraught with ten thousand wickednesses, and has utterly marred our honor and our welfare, should, on presenting a single penny, receive our worship.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: God promises a Kingdom, and is despised: the Devil helps us to hell, and he is honored!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here God, there Devil.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us see the difference of the tasks enjoined.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if there were none of these considerations in the case: if it were not, here God, there Devil; not, here one helping to a kingdom, there to a hell: the nature itself of the tasks enjoined were sufficient to induce us to comply with the former.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For what does each enjoin?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The one, the things which make glorious; the other the things which put to shame: one, the things which involve in ten thousand calamities and disgraces; the other, the things which have with them abundant refreshment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For look: the one saith, “Learn ye of Me, for I am meek and lowly of heart, and ye shall find rest unto your souls.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 29): the other saith, Be thou savage, and ungentle, and passionate, and wrathful, and more a wild beast than a man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us see which is more useful, which, I pray you, more profitable.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Speak not of this,” say you. * * * But consider that he is the devil: above all indeed, if that be shown: there is need also to undergo toils, and, on the other hand, the prize of victory will be greater.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For not he that enjoins easy tasks is the kind (κηδεμὼν) benefactor, but he that enjoins what is for our good.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since fathers also enjoin disagreeable tasks; but for this they are fathers: and so again do masters to slaves: but kidnappers and destroyers (λυμεὥνες) on the other hand, do just the reverse.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet that the commands of Christ are attended with a pleasure, is manifest from that saying.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For to what sort do you take the passionate man to belong, and to what the forbearing and meek?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Does not the soul of the (ἐκείνου) one seem to be in a kind of solitary retreat, enjoying exceeding quiet; while that of (τούτου) the other is like a market-place and tumult and the midst of cities, where great is the clamor of those going out, the noise of camels, mules, asses: of men shouting loud to those that meet them, that they may not be trodden under foot: and again, of silver-beaters, of braziers, of men thrusting and pushing this way and that and some overborne, some overbearing?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But the soul of (τούτου) the former is like some mountain-top, with its delicate air, its pure sunshine, its limpid gushing fountains, its multitude of charming flowers, while the vernal meads and gardens put on their plumage of shrubs and flowers, and glance with rifling waters: and if any sound is heard there, it is sweet, and calculated to affect the ear with a sense of much delight.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For either the warbling birds perch on the outermost spray of the branching trees, and cicadas, nightingales and swallows, blended in one harmony, perform a kind of concerted music; or the zephyr gently stirring the leaves, draws whistling tones from pines and firs, resembling oft the notes of the swan: and roses, violets, and other flowers, gently swayed, and (κυανίζοντα) dark-dimpling, show like a sea just rippled over with gentle undulations.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay, many are the images one might find.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus, when one looks at the roses, one shall fancy that he beholds in them the rainbow; in the violets a waving sea; in the lilies, the sky.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But not by the spectacle alone, and the beholding, does such an one then cause delight: but also in the very body of him that looks to the meadow, rather it refreshes him, and causes him to breathe freely, so that he thinks himself more in heaven than on earth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There is withal a sound of a different kind, when water from the mountain-steep, borne by its own force through ravines gently plashes over its pebbly bed with lulling noise, and so relaxes our frame with the pleasurable sensations, as quickly to draw over our eyes the soft languor of slumber.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You have heard the description with pleasure: perhaps also it has made you enamored of solitude.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But sweeter far than this solitude is the soul * * of the long-suffering.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it was not for the sake of describing a meadow, nor for the sake of making a display of language, that we have broached this similitude: but the object was, that, seeing how great is the delight of the long suffering, and how, by converse with a long suffering man, one would be far more both delighted and benefited, than by frequenting such spots, ye may follow after such men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For when not even a breath of violence proceeds from such a soul, but mild and engaging words, then indeed does that gentle softness of the zephyr find its counterpart: entreaties also, devoid of all arrogance, but forming the resemblance to those winged warblers,—how is not this far better?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For not the body is fanned by the soft breeze of speech; no, it refreshes our souls heated and glowing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: A physician, by ever so great attention, could not so speedily rid a man of the fever, as a patient man would cool, by the breath of his own words, a person who was passionate and burning with wrath.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And why do I speak of a physician?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not even iron, made red-hot and dipped into water, so quickly parts with its heat, as does the passionate man when he comes in contact with the soul of the long-suffering.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But as, if it chance that singing birds find their way into the market, they go for nothing there, just so is it with our precepts when they light upon souls addicted to wrathful passions.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Assuredly, sweeter is gentleness than bitterness and frowardness.—Well, but the one was God’s bidding, the other the devil’s.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you see that it was not for nothing that I said, even if there were no devil or God in the case, the things enjoined would be enough in themselves to (ἀποστἥσαι) revolt us?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the one is both agreeable to himself, and serviceable to others, the other displeasing to himself, and hurtful to others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing is more unpleasant than a man in a passion, nothing more noisome, more odious, more shocking, as also nothing more pleasing than one who knows not what it is to be in a passion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Better dwell with a wild beast than with a passionate man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the beast, when once tamed, abides by its law; but the man, no matter how often you have tamed him, again turns wild, unless however he should of himself settle down into some such habit (of gentleness).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as a bright sunny day and winter with all its gloom, so are the soul of the angry and that of the gentle.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: However, let us at present look not to the mischievous consequences resulting to others, but to those which affect the persons themselves: though indeed it is also no slight mischief (to one’s self) to cause ill to another, for the present, however, let that be the consideration.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What executioner with his lash can so lacerate the ribs, what red-hot lancets (ὀβελίσκοι) ever so pierced the body, what madness can so dispossess a man of his natural reason, as anger and rage do?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I know many instances of persons engendering diseases by giving loose to anger: and the worst of fevers are precisely these.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if they so injure the body, think of the soul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For do not argue that you do not see the mischief, but rather consider, if that which is the recipient of the malignant passion is so hurt, what must be the hurt sustained by that which engenders it!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Many have lost their eyes, many have fallen into most grievous disease.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yet he that bears bravely, shall endure all things easily.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But, however, both such are the troublesome tasks the devil enjoins, and the wages he assigns us for these is hell.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He is both devil and foe to our salvation, and we rather do his bidding than Christ’s, Saviour as He is, and Benefactor and Defender, and speaking as He does such words, which are both sweeter, and more reverend, and more profitable and beneficial, and are both to ourselves and to those who live in our company the greatest of blessings.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing worse than anger, my beloved, nothing worse than unseasonable wrath.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It will not have any long delay; it is a quick, sharp passion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Many a time has a mere word been blurted out in anger, which needs for its curing a whole lifetime, and a deed been done which was the ruin of the man for life.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the worst of it is this, that in a little moment, and by one act, and by a single word, full oft has it cast us out from the possession of eternal good, and brought to nought a world of pains.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore I beseech you to do all you can to curb this savage beast.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus far, however, I have spoken concerning meekness and wrath; if one should take in hand to treat of other opposites, as covetousness and the mad passion for glory, contrasted with contempt of wealth and of glory; intemperance with sobriety; envy with benevolence; and to marshal them each against its opposite, then one would know how great the difference.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Behold how from the very things enjoined it is plainly shown, that the one master is God, the other the devil!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why then, let us do God’s bidding, and not cast ourselves into bottomless pits; but while there is time, let us wash off all that defiles the soul, that we may attain unto the eternal blessings, through the grace and mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ, with Whom to the Father and Holy Ghost together be glory, power, honor, now and ever, and world without end.
